op.name: delete
op.notes: Return array with sub-arrays deleted along axis. Cost: num deleted.
op.summary: Return a new array with sub-arrays along an axis deleted. For a one dimensional array, this returns those entries not returned by `arr[obj]`.

blocks.text:
Return a new array with sub-arrays along an axis deleted. For a one
dimensional array, this returns those entries not returned by
`arr[obj]`.

items.name: obj
items.type: slice, int, array-like of ints or bools

desc_blocks.text: Indicate indices of sub-arrays to remove along the specified axis.

desc_blocks.name: versionchanged:: 1.19.0
desc_blocks.body:
Boolean indices are now treated as a mask of elements to remove,
    rather than being cast to the integers 0 and 1.
desc_blocks.raw:
.. versionchanged:: 1.19.0
    Boolean indices are now treated as a mask of elements to remove,
    rather than being cast to the integers 0 and 1.

desc_blocks.text:
The axis along which to delete the subarray defined by `obj`.
If `axis` is None, `obj` is applied to the flattened array.

A copy of `arr` with the elements specified by `obj` removed. Note
that `delete` does not occur in-place. If `axis` is None, `out` is
a flattened array.

blocks.text: Often it is preferable to use a boolean mask. For example:

blocks.code:
>>> arr = np.arange(12) + 1
>>> mask = np.ones(len(arr), dtype=bool)
>>> mask[[0,2,4]] = False
>>> result = arr[mask,...]

blocks.text:
Is equivalent to ``np.delete(arr, [0,2,4], axis=0)``, but allows further
use of `mask`.
insert — Insert elements into an array.
append — Append elements at the end of an array.
